explanation: The requirement specifies that the state transition from PULLUP to TRANSITION can only occur when both the 'supported' and 'good' atomic propositions are true. In LTL, this is best captured by stating that globally, whenever the autopilot changes from the PULLUP state to the TRANSITION state (i.e., state_is_PULLUP holds now, and in the next step state_is_TRANSITION holds and state_is_PULLUP does not), then at the time of transition ('now'), both 'supported' and 'good' must be true. This guards every such transition with these two conditions. Previous LTL properties commonly fail to capture the specific point at which the system is allowed to change states, or they might impose the conditions (supported & good) at all times during TRANSITION, rather than exactly during the transition itself. The provided property focuses strictly on the instant of state change.

The requirement states that the autopilot should change its state from PULLUP to TRANSITION only if the system is supported and the sensor data is good. In terms of the provided atomic propositions, 'state_is_PULLUP' and 'state_is_TRANSITION' refer to the autopilot being in the respective states. 'supported' and 'good' correspond to the required environmental/systemal conditions.

To express the transition (state) from PULLUP to TRANSITION under the conditions, we use the globally operator to always require that whenever the system is in the PULLUP state and (supported & good) holds, eventually the system will be in the TRANSITION state.

This is captured in LTL as: G((state_is_PULLUP & supported & good) -> F state_is_TRANSITION)

This property ensures that every time the system is in state PULLUP with the correct conditions, a TRANSITION will eventually occur. This most closely matches the natural language statement, translating 'change state from PULLUP to TRANSITION when ...' as a guarantee that the transition will eventually happen when the preconditions are met.

The requirement says that the autopilot shall change states from PULLUP to TRANSITION when the system is supported and sensor data is good. In terms of atomic propositions, 'state_is_PULLUP' means the autopilot is in the PULLUP state, and 'state_is_TRANSITION' means the autopilot is in the TRANSITION state. The transition should only occur when both 'supported' and 'good' are true. 

The LTL property G((state_is_PULLUP & supported & good & X state_is_TRANSITION) -> (state_is_PULLUP & supported & good)) means: on any step where the autopilot is in PULLUP, 'supported' and 'good' are true, and in the next state it is in TRANSITION, then the transition must have occurred as a result of the conjunction of these conditions. This property ensures that a transition from PULLUP to TRANSITION only occurs if the system was supported and the sensor data was good at the moment before the transition. The property prevents illegal transitions due to bad support or faulty sensor data. 

Alternative formulations without the 'X' temporal operator would fail to properly capture the requirement that the transition occurs in the next state and in response to the specified conditions, not just coincidentally or at some arbitrary time.
output_LTL: G(((state_is_PULLUP & supported & good) & X state_is_TRANSITION) -> (state_is_PULLUP & supported & good))

explanation: The natural language requirement specifies that a transition from the PULLUP state to the TRANSITION state must only occur when the system is supported and when the prior sensor data is good. In terms of LTL, we express this as: Whenever the autopilot is in the PULLUP state and in the next step will be in the TRANSITION state, then at the current step, 'supported' and 'good' must both hold (i.e., be true). This is expressed as a globally enforced safety property on state transitions. Previous LTL properties that did not make this explicit (for changing from PULLUP to TRANSITION only when supported and good are true), or that merely stated that supported and good should be true when in the TRANSITION state, would not capture the 'transition must only occur in the presence of these conditions.' Thus, we use: G((state_is_PULLUP & X state_is_TRANSITION) -> (supported & good))

The requirement specifies a state transition in the autopilot: it should change state from PULLUP to TRANSITION only when both 'supported' and 'good' are true. In terms of LTL, 'state_is_PULLUP' being true followed by 'state_is_TRANSITION' being true, with the condition that at the time of transition both 'supported' and 'good' are true. The property should ensure that whenever there is a transition from PULLUP to TRANSITION, the required conditions are satisfied.

This can be phrased as: globally, whenever the system leaves the PULLUP state and enters the TRANSITION state in the next time step, then both 'supported' and 'good' must be true at the moment of transition. We look for a location where 'state_is_PULLUP' is true and in the next state, 'state_is_TRANSITION' is true. At such a step, we require 'supported' & 'good' to be true.

This avoids allowing transitions from PULLUP to TRANSITION when the necessary conditions are not satisfied. Previous LTL properties that do not explicitly check the satisfaction of 'supported' and 'good' at the point of transition, or do not precisely encode the state change between PULLUP and TRANSITION, would fail to capture the requirement.

The requirement states that the autopilot can transition from PULLUP to TRANSITION only when two conditions are true: 'supported' and 'good'. The atomic propositions provided define 'state_is_PULLUP' for the PULLUP state, and 'state_is_TRANSITION' for TRANSITION. Therefore, a correct LTL translation should specify: if at any step, autopilot leaves PULLUP and enters TRANSITION, then at that moment 'supported' and 'good' were both true.

The most natural translation is to express that whenever there is a rising edge where 'state_is_PULLUP' becomes false and 'state_is_TRANSITION' becomes true (i.e., a transition from PULLUP to TRANSITION), it must be that both 'supported' and 'good' are true at that step. In LTL, this is typically written as: globally, whenever (currently PULLUP and next TRANSITION), supported and good must hold at that step.

Previous LTLs might get this wrong by not specifying the precise step of transition, or by not requiring both support conditions at the transition moment—this implementation is careful to do both.

The natural language states that the autopilot should change from PULLUP to TRANSITION when the system is supported and the sensor data is good. We have atomic propositions: 'state_is_PULLUP' (true when in PULLUP), 'state_is_TRANSITION' (true when in TRANSITION), 'supported' (system is supported), and 'good' (sensor data is good). In LTL, this can be formulated as: globally, whenever the current state is PULLUP and both supported and good become true, then eventually the state will become TRANSITION. This can be expressed as: G((state_is_PULLUP & supported & good) -> F state_is_TRANSITION). This property enforces that, whenever all three conditions are true simultaneously, a transition to the TRANSITION state will eventually follow. Previous translations might have omitted the requirement that the trigger is the simultaneous assertion of PULLUP, supported, and good, or might not have used the intermediate 'eventually' temporal operator, but this formula ensures both causality and necessity.

The natural language specifies that the system should transition from the 'PULLUP' state to the 'TRANSITION' state when the atomic propositions 'supported' and 'good' are both true. To represent a state change from 'PULLUP' to 'TRANSITION', we can require that whenever 'state_is_PULLUP' holds and eventually both 'supported' and 'good' hold, then eventually the system should enter the 'state_is_TRANSITION'. The LTL encodes that globally, if the system is in 'PULLUP' and both 'supported' and 'good' are true, then eventually the state changes to 'TRANSITION'. This property precisely encodes the required triggering condition and result, referencing the atomic propositions. Previous LTL encodings might miss requiring both conditions ('supported' and 'good') simultaneously, or might encode only eventualities without reference to current state, or might allow the transition to happen outside the specified condition window. This LTL ensures it only happens when required.

The requirement specifies that a state transition in the autopilot should occur from PULLUP to TRANSITION only when both 'supported' is true and 'good' is true—representing healthy system and good sensor data. We want to capture that whenever we leave state_is_PULLUP and immediately enter state_is_TRANSITION, then at that transition both 'supported' and 'good' were true. The natural LTL way to express such a constraint is: whenever there is a transition from state_is_PULLUP being true to state_is_TRANSITION being true in the next step, then supported & good must hold at the moment of the transition. We express the transition using 'state_is_PULLUP & X state_is_TRANSITION'. The implication ensures this transition is only allowed under the right conditions, so globally, every such transition is guarded by the preconditions.

The property describes that whenever the autopilot changes its state from PULLUP to TRANSITION, this should only happen when the system is supported and sensor data is good. The propositions state_is_PULLUP and state_is_TRANSITION indicate the autopilot's current state. The formula ensures that a transition from PULLUP to TRANSITION (i.e., being in PULLUP and, in the next step, being in TRANSITION) can only occur if supported and good are both true at the moment of transition. This is captured as: it is always the case that if the system is in PULLUP and, in the next step, in TRANSITION, then supported and good must hold. This strictly encodes the natural language requirement in terms of atomic propositions and LTL operators, with no direct numerical comparisons or implication of other states. Previous LTLs may fail if they don't directly relate the state transition with the required conditions at the moment of transition.
